Academic Task 1

IELTS Academic · Task 1

Your Complete Visual Writing Guide

Master data description, visual analysis, and precise academic language for Band 7+ success. Learn to describe graphs, charts, tables, processes, and maps with confidence.

⏱ 20 minutes recommended
✍ Minimum 150 words
🎯 One-third of Writing score
📊 Overview with key features

📋 How Your Task 1 is Scored

25%

Task Response

Fully addresses all parts of the question? Clear overview present? Ideas extended and supported with relevant examples?

25%

Coherence & Cohesion

Logical paragraphing? One central idea per paragraph? Cohesive devices used naturally, not mechanically?

25%

Lexical Resource

Range of data-description vocabulary? Precise data language? Avoids repetition through varied language?

25%

Grammatical Range & Accuracy

Mix of simple and complex sentences? Wide range of structures? Few errors that don't impede communication?

🚀 Quick Start

Jump to the section you need right now. Buttons are grouped by category:

📘 Foundation
📚 Language Banks
🎯 Practice & Topics
💡 Strategy

⭐ The 5 Golden Rules

  • Always include an overview paragraph — the overview summarising main features is the single most important paragraph. Without it, you cannot score above Band 5.
  • Select key features, not every detail — examiners want you to identify the 3-5 most important trends/comparisons, not describe every single data point.
  • Include specific data in body paragraphs — always cite actual numbers, percentages, or dates from the visual. Vague descriptions like "it went up" are not enough.
  • Paraphrase the prompt in your introduction — never copy the prompt word-for-word. Use synonyms and restructuring: "The graph illustrates..." not "The graph shows..."
  • Do NOT give your opinion — Task 1 is purely descriptive. Never write "I think", "In my opinion", or "This is good/bad". Just report what you see.
Foundation

The 4-Paragraph Report Structure

A consistent, logical structure is the backbone of every high-band Task 1 response. The exact content of body paragraphs shifts by visual type, but this skeleton works for all of them.

🏗️ Paragraph-by-Paragraph Breakdown

Paragraph 1 — Introduction (~30 words · 2 min)

Paraphrase the prompt + state what the visual shows

Sentence 1: paraphrase the prompt (e.g., "The line graph illustrates..."). Sentence 2: state what the visual shows in general terms. Do NOT include specific data here. The examiner should know what you are describing by the end of paragraph 1.

🔁 "The graph shows…" → "The line graph illustrates…" / "The chart compares…" — never copy the prompt word-for-word.
Paragraph 2 — Overview ⭐ MOST IMPORTANT

Summarise the main features — NO specific numbers

This is the MOST IMPORTANT paragraph in Task 1. Write 1-2 sentences summarising the MAIN TRENDS or FEATURES — the "big picture". Do NOT include specific numbers, dates, or percentages here. The overview is what separates Band 5 from Band 7+. Start with "Overall,..." or "It is clear that...".

⚠️ A report with no overview paragraph cannot score above Band 5 in Task Achievement. This is the most important paragraph in Task 1.
Paragraph 3 — Body 1: Key Features (~50 words · 6 min)

First group of key features WITH specific data

Describe the first group of key features in detail. Include SPECIFIC DATA POINTS — numbers, percentages, dates. Group related information together (e.g., all the upward trends, or the two biggest categories). Use comparison language ("whereas", "by contrast") and data verbs ("rose", "fell", "accounted for").

📌 Always cite specific data points — "rose from 10% to 45%" not just "increased". Vague descriptions without numbers cap your score at Band 5.
Paragraph 4 — Body 2: More Key Features (~40 words · 5 min)

Second group of key features WITH specific data

Describe the remaining key features. Include SPECIFIC DATA POINTS. If there are two visuals (mixed type), this paragraph often covers the second visual. Use comparison and contrast language to connect ideas. Do NOT add a separate conclusion paragraph — the overview already serves this purpose in Task 1.

🔗 Use "Turning to…" or "Looking at…" to transition between visuals or data groups. Do NOT write a separate conclusion — the overview already serves this purpose.

✍️ Sample Structured Response Band 7

Prompt: "The line graph shows the consumption of three types of fast food in the UK from 1970 to 2020." Colours indicate: overview data points linking

Introduction (~30 words · 2 min): The line graph illustrates changes in the consumption of three types of fast food — pizza, fish and chips, and hamburgers — in the UK over a fifty-year period from 1970 to 2020.

Overview (~20 words · 1 min): Overall, it is clear that hamburger and pizza consumption increased significantly over the period, while fish and chips declined after an initial rise. By 2020, pizza had become the most popular of the three.

Body 1 (~50 words · 6 min): In 1970, fish and chips was the most consumed fast food at approximately 300 grams per week. However, it peaked at about 500 grams in 1985 before declining steadily to just 100 grams by 2020. Meanwhile, hamburger consumption rose from around 100 grams in 1970 to a peak of 400 grams in 1995, before stabilising at approximately 300 grams.

Body 2 (~50 words · 6 min): Turning to pizza, consumption was initially the lowest at roughly 50 grams, but it grew steadily throughout the period. It surpassed fish and chips in 1995 and hamburgers in 2010, reaching approximately 350 grams per week by 2020, making it the most popular fast food of the three.

✅ Ideal Word Count

Minimum: 150 words (penalties if below)
Ideal: 170–190 words
Maximum: No hard limit, but 220+ often means you are describing every detail instead of selecting key features

Quality over selection. A focused 170-word report with 3-4 key features and specific data beats a rambling 250-word report that describes everything.

🚫 What NOT to Include

  • Your personal opinion — Task 1 is purely descriptive, never write "I think" or "This is good/bad"
  • Every single data point — select only the 3-5 key features, not every number on the visual
  • A conclusion paragraph — the overview already serves this purpose in Task 1
  • Memorised "template" sentences that don't fit the specific visual
  • Vague descriptions without specific data ("it went up" instead of "it rose from 10% to 45%")
  • Reasons, causes, or explanations — just describe WHAT the data shows, not WHY

🧩 Planning Your Report (3 minutes)

  • Step 1 — Identify the visual type: Line graph, bar chart, pie chart, table, process diagram, map, or mixed. This determines your approach.
  • Step 2 — Identify the overview: What are the 1-2 biggest trends or features? Write this mentally before you start — it is the most important part of your report.
  • Step 3 — Select 3-5 key features: Do NOT try to describe every detail. Pick the highest, lowest, biggest changes, and most striking comparisons.
  • Step 4 — Note specific data points: Jot down the actual numbers, percentages, or dates for each key feature. You will need these in your body paragraphs.
  • Step 5 — Group key features: Decide which features go in Body 1 and which go in Body 2. Group related data together (e.g., all upward trends in one paragraph).
Visual Types

All 7 Task 1 Visual Types

Each visual type requires a different approach to analysis and description. Learn to identify the type, then apply the specific strategies, language, and structures for each. Click through the tabs above to explore all seven types.

📈

Line Graphs

Line graphs show how data changes over time. They are the most common Task 1 visual type. The x-axis usually represents time (years, months) and the y-axis represents the quantity being measured. You may see single lines or multiple lines comparing different categories.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for a graph with points connected by lines, usually with time on the horizontal axis. The prompt will say "The line graph shows..." or "The graph below illustrates changes in...".

🎯 Strategies

  • Identify the overall trend for each line (upward, downward, fluctuating, stable)
  • Note the starting and ending points for each line
  • Identify where lines cross or overtake each other
  • Look for significant peaks, troughs, or sudden changes
  • Group lines with similar trends together in your description

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Describing every single point on the line instead of key features
  • Forgetting to include an overview paragraph
  • Not comparing the different lines to each other
  • Using present tense for past data
  • Missing units (millions, %, thousands, etc.)

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase the prompt + state what the graph shows.
Overview: Summarise the main trends (e.g., "Overall, all categories showed an upward trend...").
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe the first 1-2 lines in detail with specific data points.
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe the remaining line(s) with data, and make comparisons.

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The graph below gives information about telephone subscribers per 1000 inhabitants, Africa 1995-2004. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Model Answer
The line chart illustrates the subscription rates of landline and mobile services in Africa between 1995 and 2004.
Overall, while fixed-line telephony experienced only a modest and steady increase, mobile subscriptions, after a slow start, grew rapidly from the late 1990s onwards and eventually overtook landlines to become the dominant form of communication.
From 1995 to 2000, landline usage rose gradually, climbing from 1.7 subscribers per 100 inhabitants to just under 3. This growth, however, was relatively limited compared with that of mobile phones. In the same period, mobile subscriptions began at a negligible 0.06% and remained almost unchanged until 1997, before rising sharply to surpass landlines at 3.1% in 2001.
After 2001, the number of fixed-line subscribers leveled off, finishing at around 3.1% in 2004. By contrast, mobile usage surged dramatically during the same period, nearly tripling to reach 8.8% by the end of the decade, thereby consolidating its position as the leading communication service in Africa.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 173 words
📊

Bar Charts

Bar charts compare quantities across different categories. They can show data at a single point in time or changes over time. Bars can be vertical (column chart) or horizontal, and can be grouped or stacked.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for rectangular bars of different heights/lengths. The prompt will say "The bar chart shows..." or "The chart compares...". Categories are on one axis and quantities on the other.

🎯 Strategies

  • Identify the highest and lowest bars in each category
  • Look for significant differences between categories
  • Note any patterns (e.g., decreasing order, grouping)
  • Compare grouped bars within each cluster
  • Use comparative language: "the highest", "nearly double", "significantly more"

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Listing every bar's value instead of highlighting key comparisons
  • Not comparing across groups or categories
  • Forgetting the overview
  • Using the wrong tense for the time period shown

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase + state what the chart compares.
Overview: Identify the most striking feature (highest, lowest, biggest difference).
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe the highest/most significant categories with data.
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe the remaining categories and make comparisons.

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The chart below shows the number of international applicants to the universities of one European country. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Model Answer
The bar chart illustrates how many international students applied to universities located in a particular European country between 2004 and 2008.
Overall, there was a decrease in the number of applicants from China, while the US and Japan saw an increase. Notably, by the end of the period, significantly more US students applied to the universities compared to those from other countries. In contrast, Russia had the fewest applicants, though its numbers remained relatively stable throughout.
In the first year, the number of Chinese applicants was by far the highest, exceeding 25,000. This figure had declined steadily to approximately 18,000 by 2006, followed by a leveling off in 2007 and then a slight drop to 15,000 in the final year.
In contrast, a continual upward trend was observed in US applicant numbers, starting at roughly 13,000, reaching parity with China in 2006, before rising considerably to finish at China’s initial figure of over 25,000. Applications from Japan also increased over the period, albeit at a much lower rate, growing minimally from around 6,000 to 10,000 at the end of the timeframe.
Lastly, the universities in the country consistently received the fewest applications from Russian students, with their figures hovering around the 3,000 mark over the period in question.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 216 words
🥧

Pie Charts

Pie charts show proportions of a whole, with each "slice" representing a percentage or fraction. They are useful for showing how something is divided into categories. You may see a single pie chart or two pie charts for comparison.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for a circle divided into segments. The prompt will say "The pie chart shows..." or "The pie charts compare...". Each segment represents a proportion of 100%.

🎯 Strategies

  • Identify the largest and smallest segments
  • Use fractions and percentages: "just over half", "approximately a quarter"
  • Compare segments: "more than double", "roughly equal"
  • If two pie charts, compare how proportions changed between them
  • Use the phrase "accounted for" or "made up"

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Listing every percentage without comparing
  • Not identifying the biggest/smallest segments in the overview
  • Forgetting that segments add up to 100%
  • Using "amount" instead of "proportion" or "percentage"

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase + state what the pie chart(s) show.
Overview: Identify the largest and smallest segments.
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe the largest segments with percentages.
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe the smaller segments and make comparisons (or describe the second pie chart).

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The charts below show the main reasons why students chose a particular university in the UK in 1997 and 2007. Provide an overview of the information by identifying and describing the key details, and include comparisons where appropriate.
Model Answer
The two pie charts illustrate the primary factors influencing students’ choice of university in the UK in the years 1997 and 2007.
Overall, while “suitable degree courses” remained the most significant reason across both years, notable changes occurred in other areas such as the importance of proximity to home and social activities. Students appeared to value academic-related factors more in 2007 than in 1997.
In 1997, 35% of students chose a university based on suitable degree courses, making it the most common reason. This was followed by quality of resources (21%) and good sports and social activities (19%). Quality of teaching accounted for 15%, and only 10% selected a university due to its closeness to their parental home.
A decade later in 2007, the popularity of “suitable degree courses” increased to 37%, and quality of teaching also became more valued, rising slightly to 18%. However, “good sports and social activities” dropped to 6%, showing a decreased emphasis on extracurricular offerings. Interestingly, the percentage of students who prioritized proximity to home rose to 22%, indicating a shift in student preferences toward convenience.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 181 words
📋

Tables

Tables present data in rows and columns. They can show a wide range of data across multiple categories and time periods. Tables require careful reading to identify the most significant trends and comparisons.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for a grid of data with rows and columns, usually with headers. The prompt will say "The table shows..." or "The table gives information about...".

🎯 Strategies

  • Read the row and column headers carefully to understand what the data represents
  • Identify the highest and lowest values in each row/column
  • Look for trends across rows or down columns
  • Group related data together in your description
  • Use precise data: exact numbers, percentages, or amounts

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Describing every cell in the table — select key features only
  • Not making comparisons between rows or columns
  • Missing the overview
  • Confusing rows and columns

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase + state what the table shows.
Overview: Summarise the main pattern (e.g., "Overall, X was consistently the highest...").
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe the most significant row or column with data.
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe the remaining data and make comparisons.

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The table below gives information about employment patterns for men and women in New Zealand in two separate years. Provide an overview of the information by identifying and describing the key details, and include comparisons where appropriate.
Model Answer
The table provides data on employment patterns in New Zealand across six sectors for both male and female workers in the years 1993 and 2003.
Overall, women were more dominant in the services and transport sector in 1993, but by 2003, their presence had become more balanced across multiple sectors. Men, on the other hand, continued to show higher participation in traditionally male-dominated sectors such as agriculture, building, and manufacturing, though there were some changes over the decade.
In 1993, the majority of women (53%) worked in services and transport, while only 22% of men were in this field. However, by 2003, female participation in this sector dropped to 36%, while male involvement rose to 31%, indicating a shift toward more gender balance. The business sector saw the most dramatic increase in female employment, rising from just 1% in 1993 to 22% in 2003, while male involvement also increased to 25%.
Agriculture experienced a decline in both genders, especially men, dropping from 38% to 8%. Manufacturing also saw a decrease for women, from 25% to 12%, but remained relatively stable for men. Interestingly, the building sector, which had no female representation in 1993, saw an increase to 6% in 2003, suggesting a gradual shift in gender roles.
In contrast, social services became more prominent for both genders, especially women, whose participation rose from 13% to 18%.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 226 words
⚙️

Process Diagrams

Process diagrams show how something works or how something is made. They typically show a series of stages connected by arrows. Common topics include manufacturing processes, natural cycles, and scientific procedures.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for a diagram with stages connected by arrows, often numbered. The prompt will say "The diagram shows how..." or "The process illustrates...".

🎯 Strategies

  • Identify the starting and ending points of the process
  • Count the number of stages
  • Use sequencing language: "First", "Next", "Then", "After that", "Finally"
  • Use passive voice: "is heated", "is transported", "are mixed"
  • Describe what happens at each stage, not just the stage names

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Forgetting the overview (state how many stages and the start/end)
  • Using active voice instead of passive
  • Missing stages or describing them out of order
  • Not using sequencing connectors

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase + state what the diagram shows.
Overview: State the number of stages and the start/end points.
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe the first half of the process in order.
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe the second half of the process in order.

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The diagram below illustrates the various stages involved in a modern water purification and distribution system for a city. Summarize the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Model Answer
The provided diagram delineates the multi-faceted procedure through which raw water is treated and subsequently distributed to an urban population.
Overall, the system comprises a sequential series of eight distinct stages, beginning with the extraction of water from a natural source and culminating in its delivery to residential and industrial consumers. The process is characterized by a transition from mechanical filtration to chemical disinfection.
Initially, water is drawn from an underground aquifer or a reservoir via a high-pressure intake pump. It then enters a sedimentation tank where large debris and suspended solids are allowed to settle. Following this, the water is channeled through a sand and gravel filtration bed, a crucial step designed to remove finer impurities. Once filtered, the water undergoes carbon activated treatment to eliminate organic contaminants and improve clarity.
In the final phases, the water is transferred to a chlorination chamber where it is chemically sterilized to eradicate pathogenic bacteria. Subsequent to disinfection, the potable water is pumped into a pressurized storage tower, which ensures a consistent flow rate. From this elevated tank, the water is distributed through an underground pipe network to individual households and commercial facilities. Finally, used water is collected and directed toward a wastewater treatment plant for recycled use, thus completing the cycle.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 210 words
🗺️

Maps

Map questions show the layout of a place at two different times, or show proposed future changes. You need to describe what changed between the two maps — what was built, demolished, expanded, or converted.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for two maps of the same area, usually labelled with years. The prompt will say "The maps show the changes that took place in [place] between [year1] and [year2]."

🎯 Strategies

  • Identify the most significant change for the overview
  • Use directional language: "to the north", "in the south-east", "along the river"
  • Use change verbs: "was built", "was demolished", "was replaced by", "was converted into"
  • Group changes by location (north, south, centre)
  • Mention what stayed the same if relevant

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Describing each map separately instead of focusing on changes
  • Forgetting the overview
  • Not using past passive tense ("was built", not "is built")
  • Missing key changes like new roads or buildings

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase + state the place and time period.
Overview: Summarise the main type of change (e.g., "Overall, the area was transformed from... to...").
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe changes in one part of the map (e.g., the north).
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe changes in the other part (e.g., the south).

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The maps below show Huntingdon as it is now, and how it will change in the future. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Model Answer
The maps below illustrate the current layout of Huntingdon and the proposed changes for the future.
Overall, the town is set to undergo a range of significant alterations, the most notable of which include the development of traffic infrastructure and the construction of an industrial zone.
At present, Huntingdon town centre is surrounded by a ring road that separates it from the built-up area, which consists of homes, shops, and other facilities. The ring road is connected to the A1 highway via Brampton Road, which runs east-west. The A14 highway is currently accessible only through a roundabout on the A1, located near the airfield to the north. Additionally, a railway line runs from the south through the built-up area and heads northeast, with a railway station located just south of the Brampton Road underpass.
In the future, the town centre and surrounding built-up area will remain unchanged. However, the most significant development will be the addition of a roundabout at the junction of Brampton Road and the A14, improving access to the railway station. The southeastern section of the A14 will also be rerouted to directly connect with both the ring road and the A1, although this will reduce direct access to the new roundabout. Finally, a major change will occur in the northwest, where the airfield will be replaced by an industrial zone, with two additional railway lines linking to the existing main railway line.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 236 words
🔀

Mixed Visuals

Mixed questions present two or more different types of visual (e.g., a line graph and a pie chart, or a table and a bar chart). You need to describe both visuals and, where relevant, make connections between them.

🔍 How to Recognise This Type

Look for two or more different types of visual on the same page. The prompt will say "The chart and graph show..." or "The table and pie chart illustrate...".

🎯 Strategies

  • Describe the overview covering both visuals
  • Use transition phrases: "Turning to the second chart...", "The pie chart, by contrast..."
  • Identify connections between the two visuals if they exist
  • Give roughly equal attention to both visuals
  • Don't try to describe everything — select key features from each

⚠️ Pitfalls

  • Describing one visual in detail and ignoring the other
  • Not using transition phrases between visuals
  • Forgetting the overview should cover both visuals
  • Trying to force comparisons where none exist

🏗️ Recommended Structure

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows: Paraphrase + state what both visuals show.
Overview: Summarise the main features of BOTH visuals.
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers): Describe the first visual with key data points.
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data: Describe the second visual with key data points, using "Turning to..." to transition.

✍️ Sample Band 7 Answer

Prompt
The charts below give information on the location and types of dance classes young people in a town in Australia are currently attending. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Model Answer
The pie chart provides a breakdown of the venues where dance classes are held, while the bar chart illustrates the participation in three types of dance classes among young individuals in a specific Australian town.
Overall, private studios emerge as the most popular venue for dance classes, comprising 48% of the total, followed distantly by school halls used for after-school sessions at 24%. Community halls and other venues account for nearly one-fifth of dance classes (18%), while college-based studios represent the least chosen location, making up just 10%.
Regarding the types of dance classes attended, children under the age of 11 show a predominant interest in ballet, with 600 participants, which is twice the number in the 11-16 age group. Similarly, tap dancing sees a higher participation rate among younger children, with approximately 450 attendees, compared to 420 in the older age bracket. In contrast, modern dance classes attract more students aged 11-16, with over 500 participants, whereas only 300 younger children participate in this genre.
In conclusion, the data illustrates a preference for private studios as the primary venue for dance classes, while highlighting age-based differences in the participation rates across different dance types. Younger children predominantly favor ballet and tap dancing, whereas modern dance attracts a larger proportion of older students in the surveyed Australian town.
(Word count: 243)
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 218 words

⚠️ Using Linking Words Naturally

  • Don't start every single sentence with a linking word — it reads as mechanical, not sophisticated
  • Don't use "Moreover" and "Furthermore" interchangeably in the same paragraph — vary your addition language
  • Avoid heavy connectors like "Notwithstanding this" unless you're confident in the surrounding grammar
  • A well-placed linking word inside a sentence (not just at the start) often sounds more natural: "The policy, however, failed to account for…"
Grammar & Structures

Essential Grammatical Structures

Master these patterns to improve your Grammatical Range & Accuracy score and describe data with precision and clarity.

🕐 Tense Reference Guide for Task 1

ContextTense to UseExample
Data from the past (e.g., 2000-2020)Past simple"Sales rose from 100 to 500 between 2000 and 2020."
Data that started in the past and continuesPresent perfect"Internet usage has increased significantly since 2000."
Describing a general truth shown in the visualPresent simple"The graph shows a clear upward trend."
Predictions / future projectionsFuture simple / "is expected to""The population is expected to reach 10 million by 2040."
Describing a processPresent simple passive"The clay is mixed with water and then shaped into bricks."
Describing map changesPast simple passive"The old factory was demolished and replaced by a shopping centre."

🔗 Complex Sentence Structures for Data Description

Comparison Structures (While / Whereas / By contrast)

While car usage rose steadily, cycling declined over the same period.
The figure for Japan was significantly higher than that of any other country, whereas India had the lowest.
✦ These structures are essential for comparing data points — a hallmark of Band 7+ Task 1 writing.

Time Reference Structures

Between 2000 and 2010, the population grew from 5 million to 8 million.
Over the twenty-year period, all categories showed an upward trend.
By 2020, the figure had reached 95%.
✦ Precise time references are critical for line graphs and bar charts showing changes over time.

Relative Clauses (for adding data concisely)

The USA, which had the highest figure throughout the period, reached 95% by 2020.
Food, accounting for 35% of total spending, was the largest category.
✦ Relative clauses let you pack extra data into a single sentence without starting a new one.

Nominalisation (turning verbs into nouns)

The increase in population led to greater demand for housing. (instead of "As population increased...")
The decline in smoking was evident across all age groups.
✦ Nominalisation creates a more formal, academic tone appropriate for Task 1 reports.

Passive Voice (for process diagrams and maps)

The clay is extracted from the ground and transported to the factory.
The old buildings were demolished to make way for a new road.
✦ Passive voice is essential for describing processes and map changes — focus on what happens, not who does it.

🔀 Quick Linking Words for Data Description

This is a quick reference only — for the full categorised, searchable collection, visit the Linking Words Bank.

In addition, … / Furthermore, … / Moreover, … / Similarly, … / Likewise, …

Use to add a related data point or comparison.

However, … / By contrast, … / Whereas… / On the other hand, … / Conversely, …

Use to introduce a contrasting trend or comparison.

Initially, … / Subsequently, … / Thereafter, … / Meanwhile, … / Eventually, …

Essential for describing the sequence of trends over time.

Overall, … / In general, … / It is clear that … / The most striking feature is …

Use to open your overview paragraph — the most important paragraph in Task 1.

📐 Comparatives, Superlatives & Fractions

Comparing data

The figure for Japan was significantly higher than that of India.
Sales were almost double the previous year's figure.
The proportion more than tripled over the period.

Describing proportions

A significant proportion of respondents chose the online option.
Food accounted for 35% of total spending.
Just over half of the population had internet access.
The figure stood at approximately 45%.
Strategy

Time Management (20 Minutes)

The single most underestimated skill in IELTS Writing Task 1 is time management. Most candidates who score below their potential do so not because they lack knowledge, but because they run out of time, leave essays unfinished, or skip the proofreading stage. This page gives you the exact minute-by-minute breakdown of how to use your 20 minutes, plus practice templates and strategies for each phase.

On this page

  • The 20-minute breakdown
  • Planning phase (3 min)
  • Writing phase (15 min)
  • Checking phase (2 min)
  • Pacing strategies
  • Practice schedule

⏱ The 20-Minute Breakdown

You have exactly 20 minutes for Task 1. The recommended split below has been refined through analysis of thousands of high-band reports. Deviating significantly from these proportions — particularly by skipping the overview or checking — is the single most common cause of underperformance.

3
minutes
Plan
Analyse visual, identify key features
15
minutes
Write
4 paragraphs, ~150 words
3
minutes
Check
Proofread and refine
💡 The Golden Rule: Never skip the planning phase to "save time". Candidates who plan write faster, not slower, because they never sit frozen mid-report wondering what key features to highlight. Equally, never skip the checking phase — most Band 6 to Band 7 jumps happen in those final 2 minutes, when candidates catch the small errors that drag down their Grammatical Range and Accuracy score.

📝 Phase 1: Planning (Minutes 0–3)

Use these 3 minutes to analyse the visual and plan your response. Do not start writing until you have completed all four sub-steps below.

1

Decode the visual (30 seconds)

Identify the visual type (Line Graph, Bar Chart, Pie Chart, Table, Process Diagram, Map, or Mixed). Read the prompt carefully and note the time period, units, and categories shown. Identify what the visual is about — this is essential for writing an accurate introduction.

2

Identify key features (1 minute)

Scan the visual and identify the 3-5 most important features: the highest and lowest values, the biggest changes, the most striking comparisons. These will form the basis of your overview and body paragraphs. Do NOT try to describe every detail.

3

Note specific data points (30 seconds)

Jot down the actual numbers, percentages, or dates for each key feature. You will need these specific data points in your body paragraphs. Vague descriptions like "it went up" will not score above Band 5.

4

Sketch your paragraph plan (1.5 minutes)

Write a one-line outline for each of your four paragraphs: Intro (paraphrase + what visual shows), Overview (main trend, no numbers), Body 1 (first group of key features with data), Body 2 (second group of key features with data). Select only 3-5 key features total; do not try to describe every detail.

✍️ Phase 2: Writing (Minutes 3–18)

You have 15 minutes to write approximately 150 words across 4 paragraphs. Aim for the time allocations below — if you fall behind, do not extend any single paragraph at the expense of the others.

~30 words · 2 min · Paraphrase + what visual shows
~30 words · 2 minutes · Paraphrase prompt + state what visual shows
3–5 min
~50 words · 5 min · Overview (NO numbers)
~50 words · 5 minutes · Overview: summarise main features (NO numbers)
5–10 min
~50 words · 6 min · Key features with data
~50 words · 5 minutes · Overview: summarise main features (NO numbers)
10–15 min
~40 words · 5 min · More key features with data
~40 words · 5 minutes · More key features WITH specific data points
15–18 min
⚠️ Watch out: Body paragraphs are the most common place to lose time. If you have not finished Body 1 by minute 12, wrap it up immediately and move on. A shorter but complete report always scores higher than a longer but unfinished one.

🔍 Phase 3: Checking (Minutes 18–20)

The final 2 minutes are where Band 6 reports become Band 7 reports. Most candidates finish writing, breathe a sigh of relief, and submit — leaving careless errors that drag down their Grammatical Range and Accuracy score. Use this checklist:

  • Subject-verb agreement — check that singular subjects have singular verbs and vice versa. This is the most common error in IELTS reports.
  • Articles (a, an, the) — check each noun for the correct article. Article errors are particularly common for speakers of languages that do not use articles.
  • Tense consistency — make sure you have not slipped between present, past, and future tenses within the same paragraph.
  • Spelling — look for common IELTS spelling errors: "developement" → "development", "enviroment" → "environment", "goverment" → "government".
  • Word repetition — if you have used the same word three times, find a synonym. Variety in vocabulary directly affects your Lexical Resource score.
  • Linking word placement — check that "However", "Furthermore" and similar connectors are followed by commas, and that you have not used the same connector twice in a row.
  • Key feature coverage — re-read the prompt and confirm you have described the key features. Have you included an overview? Have you used specific data points?
  • Word count — confirm you have written at least 150 words. Aim for 170–190 to describe key features properly without padding.

📊 Pacing Strategies

Even with a plan, pacing is hard. Use these strategies to stay on track:

If you fall behind

  • Shorten your introduction — it can be 1-2 sentences if necessary.
  • Drop one key feature rather than rushing all of them.
  • Never skip the overview — a report without one caps your Task Achievement score at Band 5.
  • Reduce checking time to 1 minute rather than skipping it entirely.

If you finish early

  • Do not add new data — instead, describe the existing key features more precisely.
  • Upgrade vocabulary: replace simple data words with more precise alternatives (e.g., "went up" → "rose sharply").
  • Add a comparison sentence linking two data points.
  • Check each sentence for one final error pass.

🎯 Practice Schedule

Time management is a skill that must be practised. Use this 4-week schedule to build reliable habits before exam day:

Week 1: Planning only

Take 10 visuals and practise only the 3-minute planning phase. Do not write the reports. Goal: train yourself to identify key features in 3 minutes flat.

Week 2: Untimed reports

Write 3-4 full reports with no time limit. Focus on structure, overview, and data accuracy. Goal: build the writing habit and develop your repertoire of data-description language.

Week 3: 25-minute reports

Write 3-4 reports in 25 minutes (5 extra). Goal: practise the full sequence without time pressure, internalising the rhythm of plan-write-check.

Week 4: Full 20-minute simulation

Write 4-5 reports in exactly 20 minutes, ideally at the same time of day as your exam. Use the timer in the Practice Prompts page. Goal: replicate exam conditions as closely as possible.

💡 Exam-day tip: Use the timer in the Practice Prompts page during your week 4 simulations. Hearing the alarm at the 5-minute and 1-minute marks trains you to react calmly when time is short, rather than panicking and producing rushed, error-strewn work.
Tips & Strategy

Common Mistakes & How to Fix Them

These are the most frequent errors that prevent students from reaching Band 7 in Task 1. Learn to spot and avoid each one.

❌ Mistake 1: No Overview Paragraph

The overview is the single most important paragraph in Task 1. Without it, you cannot score above Band 5 in Task Achievement, no matter how good your language is.

✗ Wrong
Describes individual data points without ever summarising the main trend or features.
✓ Correct
"Overall, it is clear that all categories showed an upward trend, with wind power showing the most dramatic rise."

❌ Mistake 2: Describing Every Single Detail

Examiners reward selection, not exhaustive description. Trying to report every number, percentage, and date leads to a report that is too long and lacks focus.

✗ Wrong
"In 2000 it was 10%, in 2001 it was 12%, in 2002 it was 14%, in 2003 it was 16%..."
✓ Correct
"Between 2000 and 2005, the figure rose steadily from 10% to 20%."

❌ Mistake 3: No Specific Data Points

Vague descriptions like "it went up" cap your score at Band 5. Examiners need to see that you can read and report specific data from the visual.

✗ Wrong
"The number increased over the period."
✓ Correct
"The number rose from 2 million in 2000 to 5 million in 2020."

❌ Mistake 4: Giving Personal Opinion

Task 1 is purely descriptive. Never write "I think", "In my opinion", or "This is good/bad". Just report what the visual shows — nothing more.

✗ Wrong
"I think this is a positive trend because more internet access is good for education."
✓ Correct
"Internet access increased from 25% to 95% between 2000 and 2020."

❌ Mistake 5: Wrong Tense

Using the wrong tense for the time period shown is one of the most common errors. Past data requires past tenses; predictions require future tenses; processes require present simple.

✗ Wrong
"In 2000, the population is 5 million." (present tense for past data)
✓ Correct
"In 2000, the population was 5 million." (past simple for past data)

❌ Mistake 6: Copying the Prompt Word-for-Word

Copying three or more consecutive words from the prompt directly into your introduction will not contribute to your Lexical Resource score. You must paraphrase.

✗ Wrong
"The graph below shows the consumption of three types of fast food in the UK from 1970 to 2020." (copied verbatim)
✓ Correct
"The line graph illustrates changes in the consumption of three types of fast food in the UK over a fifty-year period."

⏱️ Time Management in the Exam

3
minutes

Analyse the visual. Identify the type, key features, and overview.

15
minutes

Write your report: Intro, Overview, Body 1, Body 2 (~150 words).

2
minutes

Check tenses, data accuracy, and that you included an overview.

💡 Final Tips for Band 7+

  • Identify the visual type in the first 30 seconds — it determines your entire approach.
  • Always include an overview paragraph — it is the single most important part of your report.
  • Use specific data points — "rose from 10% to 45%" not just "increased".
  • Make comparisons — "whereas", "by contrast", "significantly higher than".
  • Use the correct tense — past simple for past data, present perfect for ongoing trends, future for predictions.
  • Do NOT give your opinion — Task 1 is purely descriptive.
  • Paraphrase the prompt in your introduction — never copy it word-for-word.
Practice

Practice Prompts Bank

100 real IELTS Task 1 prompts covering all seven visual types. Filter by type or topic, generate a random prompt for daily practice, or click "Reveal Answer" to see a full Band 7 model essay (introduction, body paragraphs, conclusion). Use the built-in timer and word counter to simulate real exam conditions.

Loading prompts…

No prompts match your filters. Try a different combination.

⏱ Timed Practice
20:00
0 words · Target: 150+
Practice

Band 5 → 8 Comparison

The single most instructive exercise for any IELTS candidate: read the same visual described at four different band levels. Hover over the highlighted annotations to see exactly what the examiner noticed. This page will compare all 7 visual types at 4 band levels (28 annotated essays total) once you provide the sample answers.

📊

Coming Soon

This section will feature 28 annotated sample answers (7 visual types × 4 band levels) once you provide the visuals and answers. For each visual type, you'll send 4 answers describing the same visual at Band 5, 6, 7, and 8 levels.

Needed: 28 samples (4 per visual type)
Skills

Identifying Key Features

Most Band 6 candidates do not fail on language — they fail on describing too many details instead of selecting the key features. This page teaches you how to quickly identify the 3-5 most important trends, comparisons, and data points in any Task 1 visual, with worked examples for each visual type.

On this page

  • The 5-step method
  • Worked Example: Line Graph
  • Worked Example: Bar Chart
  • Worked Example: Pie Chart
  • Worked Example: Table
  • Worked Example: Process Diagram
  • Worked Example: Map
  • Worked Example: Mixed

🔍 The 5-Step Key Feature Identification Method

Use this method in the first 3 minutes after seeing the visual. Do not skip steps. Most candidates who score below Band 7 do so because they describe every detail instead of selecting the key features that matter.

1

Identify the visual type (15 seconds)

Determine whether you are looking at a line graph, bar chart, pie chart, table, process diagram, map, or mixed visual. This determines your approach: line graphs emphasise trends over time, bar charts emphasise comparisons, pie charts emphasise proportions, tables require careful selection, process diagrams require sequencing, and maps require change description.

2

Read the axes, labels, and units (30 seconds)

Note the time period shown (e.g., 2000-2020), the units (percentages, millions, dollars, kilograms), and the categories or countries being compared. Misreading the units or time period is one of the most common causes of lost marks — always check whether the y-axis starts at zero, and whether the data is in percentages or absolute numbers.

3

Identify the overview — the 1-2 biggest trends (1 minute)

This is the single most important step. Ask yourself: "If I could only tell the examiner ONE thing about this visual, what would it be?" Look for the overall direction (upward, downward, fluctuating), the biggest category, the most striking comparison, or the main transformation. Write this mentally — it will become your overview paragraph. Do NOT include specific numbers here.

4

Select 3-5 key features with specific data (1 minute)

Now look for the specific data points that support your overview. Note the highest and lowest values, the biggest changes, where lines cross or overtake each other, the largest and smallest segments, or the most significant differences. Jot down the actual numbers — you will need these in your body paragraphs. Do NOT try to note every data point; select only the 3-5 most important ones.

5

Group key features into two body paragraphs (30 seconds)

Decide which key features go in Body 1 and which go in Body 2. Group related features together: all upward trends in one paragraph and all downward trends in another, or the first visual in Body 1 and the second visual in Body 2 for mixed questions. This ensures your report has a logical structure rather than a random list of data points.

📈 Worked Example: Line Graph

Prompt
The graph below shows the consumption of fish and some different kinds of meat in a European country between 1979 and 2004. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Line graph — shows consumption of fish and three types of meat over time. Focus on trends and where lines cross.

Step 2 — Axes/units: X-axis: years 1979-2004 (25-year period). Y-axis: grams per person per week. Four lines: beef, lamb, chicken, fish.

Step 3 — Overview: While the consumption of beef and lamb declined over the period, chicken consumption rose steadily and eventually overtook beef as the most popular meat. Fish consumption remained relatively stable at a low level.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
• Beef: started highest (~220g), declined to ~100g
• Lamb: started ~150g, declined to ~50g
• Chicken: started ~140g, rose to ~250g (overtook beef around 1989)
• Fish: stable at ~50g throughout (lowest)
• Crossover: chicken overtook beef ~1989, overtook lamb ~1980

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = beef and lamb (both declining). Body 2 = chicken (rising) and fish (stable). Use "whereas", "by contrast" for comparisons. Use past tense throughout.
Model Answer
The line graph shows the weekly per-capita consumption of fish and three other meats in a single country in Europe between 1979 and 2004.
Overall, while the consumption of both beef and lamb reduced significantly during the 25-year period, that of chicken soared. Fish consumption, on the other hand, did not change much. 
Despite a handful of large fluctuations, beef remained the most commonly consumed meat before 1989, averaging 180 to 240 grams per person per week. Consumption then dropped sharply to roughly 120 grams by the end of the period. The amount of lamb eaten every week also went into sharp decline, dropping from second place in 1979 at 150 grams to third in 2004 at roughly 70 grams. 
Chicken consumption was slightly below that of lamb in 1979, at nearly 150 grams per person per week, but surpassed it by 1980. It then skyrocketed, overtaking that of beef by 1989 and eventually reaching nearly 250 grams by 2004. In contrast, although less fish was consumed than any of the other meats throughout the period, its consumption level was fairly stable, hovering around 50 grams the entire time.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 190 words

📊 Worked Example: Bar Chart

Prompt
The chart below shows the number of households in the US by their annual income in 2007, 2011 and 2015. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Bar chart — grouped bars showing US households across 5 income brackets for 3 years (2007, 2011, 2015). Focus on comparisons between years and brackets.

Step 2 — Axes/units: X-axis: 5 income brackets (e.g., under $25k, $25k-$50k, $50k-$75k, $75k-$100k, $100k+). Y-axis: number of households (millions). 3 bars per group (2007, 2011, 2015).

Step 3 — Overview: Income distribution among US households shifted between 2007 and 2015, with the highest income bracket growing while the lowest remained the largest group throughout.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
• Under $25k: largest group in all 3 years, relatively stable
• $25k-$50k: second largest, slight decrease
• $50k-$75k: moderate, relatively stable
• $75k-$100k: grew over the period
• $100k+: grew the most over the period
• 2011 showed effects of recession (lower brackets grew)

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = lower income brackets (under $25k, $25k-$50k). Body 2 = higher income brackets ($75k-$100k, $100k+). Use "while", "whereas" to compare years within each bracket.
Model Answer
The chart classifies US households into five income brackets for 2007, 2011, and 2015.
Overall, income distribution among US households displayed similar patterns in all three years, with considerably fewer households making either $50,000 to $74,999 or $75,000 to $99,000 a year.
A substantial number of households fell into the highest income range of at least $100,000 a year in both 2007 and 2015, at around 30 million and 33 million respectively. The next two biggest groups in these two years consisted of those in the two lowest income brackets: under $25,000 and between $25,000 and $49,999. They both contained 25 million to 28 million households.
However, 2011 saw a different picture. The number of households in the highest income range, at approximately 27 million, was surpassed by the number in each of the two lowest ranges, at 28 million to 30 million.
In comparison, in all three years, only about 20 million households had an annual income of $50,000 to $74,999, and only roughly 15 million earned $75,000 to $99,999 a year.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 185 words

🥧 Worked Example: Pie Chart

Prompt
The charts below show the proportions of British students at one university in England who were able to speak other languages in addition to English, in 2000 and 2010. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Pie charts — two pie charts showing proportions of British students able to speak other languages in two different years. Focus on how proportions shifted.

Step 2 — Axes/units: Two pie charts: Year 1 and Year 2. Categories: Spanish, French, German, Another language, Two other languages, No other language. Percentages totaling 100%.

Step 3 — Overview: Spanish was the most commonly spoken language in both years, while the proportion of students who could speak only one other language changed across the period.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
• Spanish: largest proportion in both years
• French: second most common, may have decreased
• German: smaller proportion
• Another language: moderate proportion
• Two other languages: may have increased
• No other language: decreased (more students learning languages)

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = most common languages (Spanish, French). Body 2 = less common categories (German, another language, two languages, none). Use "accounted for", "represented", "made up" for proportions. Compare changes between the two years.
Model Answer
The pie charts display the percentages of British students from one English university who were able to speak languages other than English in 2000 and 2010.
Overall, the proportion of students who were able to speak additional languages went up in 2010, with Spanish being the most commonly spoken one in both years.
Those who spoke only Spanish accounted for the greatest proportions of students in both 2000 and 2010, at 30 and 35 percent respectively. With an increase to 20 and 15 percent, those who spoke another language and those who spoke two other languages became the second and third largest groups in 2010. The proportion of those who spoke no additional languages, in comparison, dropped by half to only 10 percent.
Of those who were able to speak other languages, French-only speakers were the only group whose proportion experienced a decline from 15 to 10 percent, while the proportion of German-only speakers stayed at 10 percent.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 158 words

📋 Worked Example: Table

Prompt
The table below gives information on consumer spending on different items in five different countries in 2002. Summarize the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Table — consumer spending data across 5 countries and 3 categories. Focus on which country spent most/least and which category was highest/lowest.

Step 2 — Axes/units: Rows: 5 countries. Columns: 3 spending categories (food/drinks/tobacco, clothing/footwear, leisure/education). Values in percentages of total consumer expenditure.

Step 3 — Overview: Food, drinks, and tobacco accounted for the largest proportion of consumer spending in all five countries, while leisure and education consistently represented the smallest category.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
• Food/drinks/tobacco: highest category in all countries (range ~15-32%)
• Country with highest food spending: Turkey (~32%)
• Country with lowest food spending: Sweden (~15%)
• Clothing/footwear: second highest (~5-9%)
• Italy: highest clothing spending (~9%)
• Leisure/education: lowest in all countries (~1.8-4.3%)
• Turkey: highest leisure/education (~4.3%)

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = food/drinks/tobacco (highest category) with country comparisons. Body 2 = clothing/footwear and leisure/education (lower categories) with country comparisons. Use "accounted for", "stood at", "ranged from...to".
Model Answer
The table shows, by category, consumer expenditure across five different countries in 2002.
Overall, all five countries spent the most on food, drinks and tobacco, and the least on leisure and education.
Food, drinks and tobacco accounted for the greatest proportions of consumer spending in all five countries, with Turkey (32.14%) and Ireland (28.91%) spending significantly more on these items than the other three countries (18.80% in Spain, 16.36% in Italy and 15.77% in Sweden).
Consumers in Italy, who spent 9.00% of their expenditure on clothing and footwear, were the biggest spenders on them. Expenditures in this category for the other four countries ranged from 5.40% to 6.63%.
Leisure and education were the smallest expenditure for all these countries. Even in Turkey where the spending was greatest, the figure was only 4.35%. Spain was the lowest-spending country in this category, at 1.98%.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 152 words

⚙️ Worked Example: Process Diagram

Prompt
The diagram below shows how a biofuel called ethanol is produced. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Process diagram — shows how ethanol (a biofuel) is produced. Focus on the sequence of stages from raw material to finished product.

Step 2 — Axes/units: Stages: 1) Plants/trees grow (photosynthesis) → 2) Harvesting plant material → 3) Processing/crushing into cellulose → 4) Chemical processing → 5) Ethanol produced → 6) Used as fuel for transport → 7) CO₂ emitted → 8) CO₂ absorbed by plants (cycle repeats). Cyclical process.

Step 3 — Overview: The production of ethanol is a cyclical process that begins with plants absorbing sunlight and CO₂, and ends with the ethanol being used as transport fuel, releasing CO₂ back into the atmosphere for plants to absorb again.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
• Stage 1: Plants and trees grow using sunlight + CO₂ (photosynthesis)
• Stage 2: Plant material harvested
• Stage 3: Crushed/processed into cellulose
• Stage 4: Chemical breakdown into sugars
• Stage 5: Fermentation produces ethanol
• Stage 6: Ethanol used as fuel for transport
• Stage 7: CO₂ released as exhaust
• Stage 8: CO₂ absorbed by plants (cycle repeats)

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = stages 1-4 (growing and processing plant material). Body 2 = stages 5-8 (ethanol production, usage, and the cycle completing). Use present simple passive ("is harvested", "is processed", "is produced"). Use "first", "then", "next", "finally" for sequencing.
Model Answer
The diagram illustrates the process of manufacturing ethanol, which is a biofuel.
Overall, the production of ethanol is a cyclical process that consists of harvesting plants, extracting cellulose, converting it into sugars, adding microbes to create ethanol, and carbon dioxide produced when the ethanol is burned cycling back to plants during photosynthesis.
The process begins with plants and trees using sunlight and carbon dioxide as sources of energy for growth. Once harvested, they first undergo the pre-processing stage, where a crushing machine breaks them down into small pieces to make it easier to process them further. These small pieces are then turned into cellulose, which is subsequently chemically processed to obtain sugars. From there, microbes are added to the sugars to convert them into ethanol. 
When the ethanol is burned to fuel various vehicles, including cars, trucks, and airplanes, carbon dioxide is released into the atmosphere. However, this carbon dioxide is reabsorbed by plants during photosynthesis, and the process starts again.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 161 words

🗺️ Worked Example: Map

Prompt
The plans below show the site of a farm in 1950 and the same site today. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Map — two plans of the same site at different times (1950 and today). Focus on what changed between the two periods.

Step 2 — Axes/units: Two maps side by side: 1950 farm layout vs. today's layout. Features: crop farmland, farm buildings, animal pens, roads, trees. Directional compass shown.

Step 3 — Overview: While the farmland used for crops has remained unchanged, the rest of the site has been significantly modernised. The farm buildings have been expanded and the layout reorganised.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
• Crop farmland: remained unchanged (constant feature)
• Farmhouse: expanded/renovated
• Animal pens: reorganised or reduced
• New buildings: added for modern farming
• Roads: extended or rerouted
• Trees: possibly added or removed

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = features that stayed the same + northern changes. Body 2 = southern and central changes. Use past tense for 1950 description and present tense for today. Use "was replaced by", "was converted into", "was built".
Model Answer
The plans show the layout of Beechwood Farm in 1950 and today.
Overall, while the farmland used for crops has remained untouched, several new facilities have been added to accommodate visitors.
A large part of the farm is bordered by a river and a main road. A side road extends from the main road across the farm, forming a central triangular area with two shorter side roads. These side roads used to be tracks.
Standing at the T-intersection of the main road and the long side road and facing the farm, one can see that, on the right, the farmland for fruit trees, soft fruits, and vegetables has remained unchanged, with the addition of a farm shop near the T-intersection. However, the area for sheep on the left has been replaced by a camping field, some solar panels, and two parking areas.
Moving forward into the triangular zone, one can find a new barn where the two shorter side roads meet. Continuing past the triangular zone, on the right, the old barn has been converted into holiday cottages, while the farmhouse and the children’s yard have been left as they were.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 191 words

🔀 Worked Example: Mixed

Prompt
The charts below give information about a public  library in a town called Little Chalfont. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
Step 1 — Visual type: Mixed — two different visuals on the same topic. A pie chart showing library member age groups in 2016, and a bar chart showing total library loans from 2007 to 2016. Must describe both and connect them.

Step 2 — Axes/units: Pie chart: 4 age groups (children, young adults 13-17, adults 18-64, adults 65+), percentages totaling 100%. Bar chart: Years 2007-2016 on x-axis, number of loans on y-axis (roughly 14,000-22,000).

Step 3 — Overview: Adults aged 18-64 made up the largest proportion of library members in 2016, while children's fiction and adult fiction were the two most borrowed categories. The total number of library loans broadly trended upwards over the period.

Step 4 — Key features with data:
Pie chart (age groups):
• Adults 18-64: 51% (largest group, about half)
• Children: 22% (second largest)
• Young adults 13-17: 15%
• Adults 65+: 12% (smallest group)

Bar chart (loans over time):
• 2007: ~16,000 loans (starting point)
• 2016: ~22,000 loans (ending point — overall increase of ~6,000)
• Dips in 2009 and 2013 (temporary decreases)
• Overall upward trend despite fluctuations

Step 5 — Group: Body 1 = pie chart data (age group proportions with percentages). Body 2 = bar chart data (loan trends over time with specific years and numbers). Use "Turning to the bar chart..." or "Looking at the total loans..." to transition between visuals.
Model Answer
The charts provide data on a public library in the town of Little Chalfont.
Overall, while adults aged 18-64 made up the largest proportion of library members in 2016, children’s fiction and adult fiction were the two most borrowed categories that year. The total number of library loans broadly trended upwards over the period between 2007 and 2016.
In 2016, around half (51%) of library members consisted of adults in the 18-64 age bracket. In contrast, children, young adults aged 13-17, and adults aged 65 and older represented only 22%, 15% and 12% of all members respectively. 
In that same year, children’s fiction and adult fiction were the two most popular genres. They each accounted for 38% of all library loans. Another 13% of loans were loans of adult non-fiction, compared to no more than 6% for any of the remaining four categories—children’s non-fiction, young adults, adult audio books, and children’s DVDs.
There were a total of roughly 16,000 loans from the library in 2007. This number then broadly showed an increase to around 22,000 by 2016, with a small dip in both 2009 and 2013.
Band 7+ Model Answer 📝 187 words

💡 Pro Tips

  • Always identify the overview first — before noting any specific data, decide what the "big picture" is. This is what examiners look for most.
  • The overview is what separates Band 5 from Band 7+. Without an overview paragraph, you cannot score above Band 5 in Task Achievement, no matter how good your language is.
  • Specific data points are essential — vague descriptions like "it went up" will cap your score at Band 5. Always cite actual numbers: "it rose from 10% to 45%".
  • Do NOT describe every detail. Select 3-5 key features, not 15. Examiners reward selection, not exhaustive description.
  • For mixed visuals, give roughly equal attention to both visuals. Do not describe one in detail and rush the other.
Skills

Paraphrasing Techniques

Paraphrasing is the single most underestimated skill in IELTS Writing Task 1. It directly affects two of the four marking criteria: Task Achievement (you must paraphrase the prompt in your introduction) and Lexical Resource (you must avoid repeating the same words throughout). Master these six techniques and your score will rise.

🎯 Where paraphrasing matters most

1. The introduction — your first sentence must restate the prompt in your own words; copying it directly is an instant Band 6 ceiling.
2. Describing data — vary your verbs: "rose", "increased", "climbed", "grew" instead of repeating "went up".
3. Recurring key terms — if the prompt says "the graph shows", do not write "the graph shows" ten times; vary it ("the chart illustrates", "the figure depicts", "the data presents").

Technique 1

Synonym Substitution

The simplest technique: replace key words with their synonyms. Most useful for the introduction's opening sentence, but use sparingly — relying only on synonyms produces stilted writing.

Before The graph shows the percentage of people using public transport.
After The line graph illustrates the proportion of the population travelling by public transport.
Before The chart gives information about the number of tourists.
After The bar chart provides data on the volume of visitors.
Technique 2

Active ↔ Passive Voice

Switching between active and passive voice changes sentence structure without changing meaning. Particularly useful in Task 1, where the passive voice can sound more objective and formal.

Before People consumed more fish than meat in 2000.
After Fish was consumed in greater quantities than meat in 2000.
Before The company produced 500 units in 2010.
After Five hundred units were produced by the company in 2010.
Technique 3

Clause Restructuring

Change the grammatical structure: turn a clause into a phrase, a noun into a verb, or reorder the parts of the sentence. This is the most powerful technique because it changes the rhythm of your writing.

Before The number of cars increased because the population grew.
After Population growth led to an increase in the number of cars.
Before Sales fell from 500 to 300 between 2000 and 2010.
After Between 2000 and 2010, sales experienced a decline from 500 to 300.
Technique 4

Change of Perspective

Rewrite the sentence from a different viewpoint — start with the result rather than the cause, or with the affected group rather than the actor. This often produces the most natural-sounding paraphrases.

Before The graph shows that car sales rose to 5 million in 2020.
After By 2020, car sales had climbed to 5 million, as depicted in the graph.
Before The largest proportion was accounted for by food at 35%.
After Food represented the largest share, at 35% of the total.
Technique 5

Summarising / Generalising

Replace specific phrases with more general categories. Useful when you need to compress a long prompt into a single introductory sentence.

Before The bar chart shows the number of people who travelled by train, bus, car, and bicycle in 2020.
After The bar chart illustrates the use of different modes of transport in 2020.
Before The table gives information about the population of London, Paris, Berlin, and Madrid.
After The table provides population data for four European capital cities.
Technique 6

Definition Restatement

Restate a term by describing what it means, rather than naming it. This technique forces precision and is especially useful for process diagrams and maps.

Before The diagram shows the water cycle.
After The diagram illustrates the continuous movement of water between the earth's surface and the atmosphere.
Before The maps show urbanisation.
After The maps depict the transformation of a rural area into a developed urban settlement.

⚠️ Common Paraphrasing Mistakes

  • Changing meaning while changing words — a paraphrase must preserve the original meaning exactly. "Increased" and "doubled" are not the same.
  • Overusing unusual synonyms — using "pulchritudinous" for "beautiful" makes your writing sound unnatural. Stick to words you would actually use in speech.
  • Forgetting to vary repeated key terms — if the prompt says "the graph shows", use "the chart illustrates", "the figure depicts", "the data presents" to avoid repetition.
  • Paraphrasing only the introduction — the same skill applies throughout the report. Watch for data verbs you have used more than twice.
  • Copying long phrases from the prompt — three or more consecutive words from the prompt count as copied and will not contribute to your Lexical Resource score.

🎯 Practice Exercise

Try paraphrasing the following prompt using at least three different techniques from this page:

Original: "The graph below shows the consumption of fish and meat in a European country between 1999 and 2004. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant."

Possible paraphrased opening: "The line graph illustrates changes in the amounts of fish and meat consumed in a European nation over a five-year period from 1999 to 2004." (Techniques used: synonym substitution, clause restructuring, change of perspective.)

Practice

Self-Assessment Checklist

Run through this checklist before submitting any practice report. Each item is based on the official IELTS marking criteria for Task 1. Your progress is saved automatically in your browser, so you can return to it between sessions. The score at the bottom updates live.

On this page

  • Task Achievement
  • Coherence & Cohesion
  • Lexical Resource
  • Grammatical Range & Accuracy
  • Mechanics & Presentation

🎯 Task Achievement

Does your report fully describe the key features and include a clear overview?

🔗 Coherence & Cohesion

Is your report logically organised, with clear paragraphing and smooth transitions?

📚 Lexical Resource

Have you used a wide range of data-description vocabulary accurately?

⚙️ Grammatical Range & Accuracy

Have you used a variety of grammatical structures accurately?

✍️ Mechanics & Presentation

Are the small details that examiners notice correct?

Items Checked
0
/ 35 total
0% complete · Estimated band: 5.0

📊 Score Interpretation

30-35 / 35: Your report likely meets Band 8+ standards across all four criteria.
24-29 / 35: Your report likely sits at Band 7 — solid work, focus on the unchecked items to climb higher.
17-23 / 35: Your report likely sits at Band 6 — you have the basics but need more depth and accuracy.
Below 17: Your report likely sits at Band 5 — focus on including an overview and using specific data.

Note: This checklist is for self-assessment only and does not guarantee an official band score. Use it as a learning tool to identify weaknesses, not as a substitute for professional feedback.

Language Banks

Collocations Bank

160 high-value academic collocations that lift Lexical Resource scores. Unlike individual words, collocations are partnerships — "pose a threat", "raise awareness", "bear the brunt" — that signal natural, fluent English to examiners. Filter by category, topic, or search. Mark each as learned to track your progress.
